title: The Giant Hat Disruption Equation
formula: TF = ((H × M × S) + W² + C) / (R + F)
formulaPlaintext: T F equals H times M times S plus W squared plus C, divided by R plus F.
tagline: Big hat + wrong confidence + zero format respect = legendary disruption.
derivation: A symbolic comedy craft pattern where confidence, costume, and disruption amplify, while rules and format buffer the chaos.
theoreticalEcho: Comedy incongruity, authority disruption, rule-breaking, and game-show parody structure.
culturalScope: Western sketch-comedy / game-show parody context; homage origin, not a universal claim.
temporalNote: A static snapshot of a bit escalating inside a formal structure.
reflectionPrompt: What invisible rule is being gloriously violated, and why does the format trying to survive make it funnier?
exampleApplication: A contestant answers with total confidence while the host tries to preserve order.
posterVisual: Giant hat, smirk arrows, podium, irritation chart.
visualMotif: Marquee lights, cowboy hat, red/yellow chaos panels.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not a measurement of people.
misuseWarning: Do not use as a cruelty or conflict-escalation manual.

title: The Deadpan Delta
formulaPlaintext: Deadpan power equals absurdity divided by emotional reaction.
tagline: Say the wildest thing like you are reading a parking receipt.
derivation: The formula rises when absurdity increases while visible emotional response stays low.
theoreticalEcho: Incongruity, contrast, understatement, and straight-man performance.
culturalScope: Drafted from sketch-comedy and deadpan performance traditions.
temporalNote: Works best when the audience has time to notice the emotional mismatch.
reflectionPrompt: What absurd thing becomes funnier when nobody on screen reacts like it is absurd?
exampleApplication: A character reports a disaster with the tone of someone checking a grocery list.
posterVisual: Neutral face beside a wild statement.
visualMotif: Flat expression, big absurd object, tiny reaction meter.
misuseWarning: Do not confuse emotional restraint with emotional health.

title: The Overreaction Theorem
formulaPlaintext: Drama output equals trigger times insecurity squared plus public audience, divided by calm plus sleep.
tagline: Small problem + big feelings + witnesses = instant scene.
derivation: Drama grows when a trigger is amplified by insecurity and audience pressure, then softened by calm and rest.
theoreticalEcho: Appraisal, social amplification, and narrative escalation as craft analogies.
culturalScope: Story-design and social-drama metaphor; not a clinical model.
temporalNote: Usually changes after rest, repair, or private conversation.
reflectionPrompt: What part of this scene is the actual trigger, and what part is audience amplification?
exampleApplication: A missed call becomes a betrayal because the character is tired and people are watching.
posterVisual: Theatrical hand-to-forehead figure and red alert arrows.
visualMotif: Stage curtains, warning lights, rising tension graph.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not a diagnosis.
misuseWarning: Do not use to shame strong emotions or dismiss real harm.

title: The Suspicious Silence Delta
formulaPlaintext: Drama delta equals assumptions divided by evidence.
tagline: They did not text back, so obviously it is war.
derivation: The story gets louder when assumption increases while evidence remains small.
theoreticalEcho: Appraisal, uncertainty, and narrative gap-filling as creative analogies.
temporalNote: Often resolves when direct communication arrives.
reflectionPrompt: What do we actually know, and what did the imagination add?
exampleApplication: A seen message becomes evidence in a trial nobody agreed to attend.
posterVisual: Phone bubbles, folder labeled evidence, suspicious eyes.
visualMotif: Blue chat bubbles, red suspicion arrows.
misuseWarning: Do not use to mock anxiety or real relational harm.

title: The Grief Echo Equation
formulaPlaintext: Grief echo equals loss times memory times attachment, divided by time plus witness plus ritual.
tagline: Love leaves an echo; witness helps it become bearable.
derivation: Loss and attachment deepen sadness, while time, witness, and ritual create holding structure.
theoreticalEcho: Narrative grief, memory, ritual, and social support as nonclinical design references.
culturalScope: Grief practices vary widely by culture and person.
temporalNote: Grief is nonlinear; this is a symbolic snapshot, not a schedule.
reflectionPrompt: What memory is asking to be witnessed rather than solved?
exampleApplication: A character revisits a familiar place and the memory hits before the plot moves forward.
boundaryNote: Reflection/craft metaphor only; not grief therapy.
misuseWarning: Do not rush, grade, or pathologize grief with this card.

title: The Joy Lift Equation
formulaPlaintext: Joy equals presence times gratitude times safety, plus wonder.
tagline: Joy rises when presence has room to notice the miracle.
derivation: Presence, gratitude, and safety create a receptive base; wonder adds lift.
theoreticalEcho: Awe, play, gratitude, and broaden-and-build style creative references.
culturalScope: Joy expressions are culturally and personally specific.
temporalNote: Often brief; repeated noticing can build a motif.
reflectionPrompt: What tiny thing is already glowing that the scene almost missed?
exampleApplication: A shopkeeper offers a smile for free and the whole town feels warmer.
posterVisual: Sunburst, tiny miracle icons, warmth meter.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not a requirement to be happy.
misuseWarning: Do not use joy language to erase pain or pressure positivity.

title: The Anger Boundary Engine
formulaPlaintext: Anger equals harm times violation times need, divided by clarity plus repair.
tagline: Anger is often a boundary flare asking for clarity and repair.
derivation: Harm, violation, and unmet need amplify anger; clarity and repair convert fire into signal.
theoreticalEcho: Boundary-setting, appraisal, and conflict-repair as craft references.
culturalScope: Anger expression is shaped by culture, safety, power, and history.
temporalNote: Fire can flare fast and soften when the need is named.
reflectionPrompt: What boundary or need is trying to be protected here?
exampleApplication: A character snaps, then realizes the anger is pointing to a broken agreement.
visualMotif: Red-orange fire with clear blue repair lines.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not advice for unsafe conflict.
misuseWarning: Do not use this card to justify harm, threats, or coercion.

title: The Fear Forecast Loop
formulaPlaintext: Fear equals threat times uncertainty times imagination squared, divided by safety signals plus knowledge plus support.
tagline: Fear grows in the unknown; support gives the forecast edges.
derivation: Threat and uncertainty are amplified by imagination, while safety, knowledge, and support ground the forecast.
theoreticalEcho: Threat appraisal and uncertainty as narrative/reflective design references.
culturalScope: Fear is deeply context-dependent; this is not universal or clinical.
temporalNote: Fear can shift quickly when information or support changes.
reflectionPrompt: What is known, what is imagined, and what support would make the next step safer?
exampleApplication: A dark hallway feels worse before the character learns what the sound actually is.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not clinical anxiety guidance.
misuseWarning: Do not use fear formulas to manipulate or intensify distress.

title: The Love Bridge Equation
formulaPlaintext: Love bridge equals attention times presence times trust, plus repair.
tagline: Love becomes visible where attention, presence, trust, and repair meet.
derivation: Attention and presence make care visible; trust and repair keep the bridge from collapsing.
theoreticalEcho: Attachment, repair, compassion, and relational storytelling as creative references.
culturalScope: Love has many cultural languages and relational forms.
temporalNote: The bridge strengthens through repeated trustworthy action.
reflectionPrompt: What repair would make care believable again?
exampleApplication: A character stops trying to win and starts trying to understand.
posterVisual: Bridge, heart, repair tools, warm light.
visualMotif: Rose-gold bridge lines and soft halos.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not relationship therapy.
misuseWarning: Do not use love language to excuse control, coercion, or harm.

title: The Shame Exposure Collapse
formulaPlaintext: Shame equals exposure times judgment times identity threat, divided by witness plus compassion.
tagline: Shame shrinks in the presence of safe witness.
derivation: Exposure and judgment threaten identity, while compassionate witness provides containment.
theoreticalEcho: Social emotion, belonging threat, and repair arcs as nonclinical design references.
culturalScope: Shame and honor are culturally loaded; do not universalize.
temporalNote: Can soften through trusted witness and repair over time.
reflectionPrompt: What part of the character needs safe witness instead of public judgment?
exampleApplication: A character hides after a mistake until one person stays kind and honest.
visualMotif: Mirror silver, violet shadow, warm witness light.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not mental-health advice.
misuseWarning: Do not weaponize shame or expose private pain for entertainment.

title: The Calm Grounding Loop
formulaPlaintext: Calm equals breath plus orientation plus safety, divided by threat plus noise.
tagline: Calm is not emptiness; it is enough safety to perceive.
derivation: Grounding inputs stabilize attention while threat and noise reduce available calm.
theoreticalEcho: Nervous-system literacy, learning readiness, and environmental design as broad references.
culturalScope: Calm practices vary; this is not a prescription.
temporalNote: Often works as a repeated loop, not a single switch.
reflectionPrompt: What signal of safety would help this scene or person perceive more clearly?
exampleApplication: The player enters a quiet room where sound and light give the body permission to slow down.
visualMotif: Teal, soft grids, clean breath circles.
boundaryNote: Creative metaphor only; not medical or therapeutic instruction.
misuseWarning: Do not use calm language to silence valid anger, grief, or fear.
